reference: GATE CS — Programming and Data Structures / Non-Linear Data Structures

map.condition: Height of tree / nodes in smallest AVL tree
Insert / delete one element in heap or BST
Leaves vs full nodes in binary tree

formulas.name: Leaves vs Full Nodes
formulas.plain: L = F + 1
formulas.whenToUse: Relating the number of leaves L to full (2-child) nodes F in any binary tree.
formulas.trap: This holds for ANY binary tree, not just complete or full binary trees. Nodes with exactly 1 child do not appear in the formula.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Tree

formulas.name: Maximum Nodes at Height h
formulas.plain: Max nodes = 2^(h+1) - 1  (height h, root at h=0)
formulas.whenToUse: Upper-bounding the total nodes in a binary tree of height h.

terms.means: height of the tree (root = 0, leaf level = h)

formulas.trap: Height convention varies: some sources use h=1 for root, giving max nodes = 2^h - 1. GATE typically uses h=0 for root.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Tree

formulas.name: Maximum Nodes at Level i
formulas.plain: Max nodes at level i = 2^i  (root at level 0)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the maximum number of nodes possible at a given level.
formulas.trap: Confusing level with height. Level counts from root down; height counts edges from root to deepest leaf.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Tree

formulas.name: Traversal Reconstruction Rule
formulas.plain: Inorder + Preorder (or Postorder) => unique binary tree; Preorder + Postorder alone does NOT guarantee uniqueness.
formulas.whenToUse: Deciding whether two given traversal sequences uniquely determine a binary tree.
formulas.trap: Pre-order + Post-order alone does NOT uniquely determine a binary tree (multiple trees can share both). A common GATE trap is stating they do.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Tree

formulas.name: Complete Binary Tree — Node-Height Relation
formulas.latex: \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor = h \quad \text{(height of complete BT with } n \text{ nodes)}
formulas.plain: h = floor(log2(n))  for a complete binary tree with n nodes
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the height (and thus worst-case path length) of a complete binary tree.
formulas.trap: This formula is for a COMPLETE binary tree. Arbitrary binary trees can have h up to n-1.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Tree

formulas.name: Internal vs External Nodes (Full Binary Tree)
formulas.plain: In a full binary tree (every node has 0 or 2 children): leaves = internal + 1
formulas.whenToUse: Problems about strictly binary (full) trees where every non-leaf has exactly 2 children.
formulas.trap: Same as L = F + 1 but phrased differently. Only applies when EVERY internal node has exactly 2 children.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Tree

formulas.name: BST Ordering Property
formulas.latex: \text{For every node } x: \quad \text{left subtree keys} < x.\text{key} \leq \text{right subtree keys}
formulas.plain: BST property: all keys in left subtree < node key <= all keys in right subtree
formulas.whenToUse: Validating a BST or tracing a search/insert path in GATE probe-sequence questions.
formulas.trap: Equal keys — GATE problems may place duplicates on the right (>=). Always check the problem's convention. An illegal probe sequence will violate this ordering at some step.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Search Tree

formulas.name: BST Search / Insert / Delete Complexity
formulas.plain: BST search/insert/delete: O(log n) average, O(n) worst case (skewed tree)
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing BST complexity with balanced BST (AVL) or heap operations.

terms.means: number of nodes in the BST

formulas.trap: Inserting sorted data into a BST produces a skewed (degenerate) tree with O(n) height. AVL trees guarantee O(log n) worst case.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Search Tree

formulas.name: BST Inorder Traversal Gives Sorted Output
formulas.plain: Inorder traversal of a BST produces keys in sorted (non-decreasing) order.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying valid preorder/postorder sequences of BST keys.
formulas.trap: Pre-order and post-order of a BST do NOT directly yield sorted order. Only in-order does.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Search Tree

formulas.name: Number of Distinct BSTs with n Keys
formulas.plain: Number of distinct BSTs with n keys = C_n = (1/(n+1)) * C(2n, n)  [nth Catalan number]
formulas.whenToUse: Counting the number of structurally distinct BSTs (or binary trees) for n nodes.
formulas.trap: This counts distinct BST shapes given n keys. If keys are labeled, the number of distinct BSTs equals C_n (each shape corresponds to one BST for a given sorted key set).
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Search Tree

formulas.name: Balance Factor Definition
formulas.plain: BF(x) = height(left subtree) - height(right subtree); must be in {-1, 0, 1} for AVL property.
formulas.whenToUse: Computing balance factors and checking/maintaining the AVL height-balance property.

terms.means: height of the subtree (number of edges to deepest leaf)

formulas.trap: Height of an empty subtree is -1 (by convention). Using 0 instead causes wrong balance factors at leaf nodes.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / AVL Tree

formulas.name: Minimum Nodes in AVL Tree of Height h
formulas.plain: N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1,  N(0)=1, N(1)=2  (Fibonacci-like recurrence)
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the minimum/smallest AVL tree for a given height, or inverting to find the maximum height for n nodes.

terms.means: minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height h

formulas.trap: Confusing N(h) (min nodes for height h) with max nodes (= 2^(h+1) - 1). GATE asks for minimum; the recurrence is Fibonacci-like.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / AVL Tree

formulas.name: AVL Tree Height Bound
formulas.plain: AVL height <= 1.44 * log2(n+2) - 1, so all operations are O(log n) worst case.
formulas.whenToUse: Justifying O(log n) worst-case complexity for AVL search, insert, delete.
formulas.trap: The constant 1.44 is often omitted. Remember AVL height is strictly bounded; a plain BST has no such bound.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / AVL Tree

formulas.name: AVL Rotation Cases
formulas.plain: LL => single right rotate; RR => single left rotate; LR => left rotate child then right rotate; RL => right rotate child then left rotate.
formulas.whenToUse: Restoring AVL balance after an insertion or deletion that causes |BF| > 1.

terms.means: imbalance in left child's left subtree
imbalance in right child's right subtree
imbalance in left child's right subtree
imbalance in right child's left subtree

formulas.trap: LR and RL each require TWO rotations (double rotation). Applying only one rotation leaves the tree still unbalanced.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / AVL Tree

formulas.name: Max-Heap / Min-Heap Property
formulas.plain: Max-Heap: parent >= both children  (A[floor(i/2)] >= A[i]). Min-Heap: parent <= both children.
formulas.whenToUse: Verifying a heap or tracing heapify operations. Array index i, parent at floor(i/2) (1-indexed).

terms.means: index of parent of node i

formulas.trap: 0-indexed arrays: parent of i is floor((i-1)/2). 1-indexed: parent of i is floor(i/2). GATE questions usually use 1-indexed. Mixing conventions causes wrong answers.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Heap

formulas.name: Heap Children Index Formula
formulas.latex: \text{Left child of } i = 2i, \quad \text{Right child of } i = 2i+1 \quad (1\text{-indexed})
formulas.plain: Left child of i = 2i,  Right child of i = 2i+1  (1-indexed). For 0-indexed: left = 2i+1, right = 2i+2.
formulas.whenToUse: Navigating a heap stored as an array (finding children or parent during heapify).
formulas.trap: The 0-indexed vs 1-indexed shift of +1 is a frequent off-by-one error in GATE MCQs asking for a specific child's index.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Heap

formulas.name: Heapify (Sift-Down) Complexity
formulas.plain: Heapify (sift-down one element) = O(log n)
formulas.whenToUse: Complexity of a single insert or delete-min/delete-max in a binary heap.

terms.means: number of elements in the heap

formulas.trap: Single heapify is O(log n). Build-heap is O(n) (not O(n log n)) due to the summation argument.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Heap

formulas.name: Build-Heap (Heapify All Internal Nodes)
formulas.plain: Building a heap from an unordered array of n elements costs Theta(n).
formulas.whenToUse: Heap construction from scratch — the linear-time algorithm that sifts down from floor(n/2) down to 1.
formulas.trap: Naively inserting n elements one-by-one costs O(n log n). The bottom-up build-heap achieves O(n). GATE often matches complexities and this O(n) result is a standard pairing.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Heap

formulas.name: Heap Sort Complexity
formulas.plain: Heap sort: O(n log n) time, O(1) extra space, NOT stable.
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing heap sort with merge sort or quick sort on time/space/stability.
formulas.trap: Heap sort is NOT stable. Merge sort is stable and also O(n log n) but needs O(n) extra space.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Heap

formulas.name: Number of Internal Nodes in a Heap
formulas.plain: A complete binary tree (heap) with n nodes has floor(n/2) internal (non-leaf) nodes and ceil(n/2) leaves.
formulas.whenToUse: Build-heap starts sifting from floor(n/2). Counting leaves vs internal nodes in heap problems.
formulas.trap: Leaf count is ceil(n/2), not floor(n/2). These differ for odd n.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Binary Heap

formulas.name: Priority Queue Operation Complexities
formulas.plain: Priority Queue (binary heap): Insert O(log n), Extract-Min/Max O(log n), Peek (find min/max) O(1).
formulas.whenToUse: Analyzing algorithms that use a priority queue (e.g., Dijkstra, Prim, Huffman coding).

terms.means: number of elements in the priority queue

formulas.trap: Peek (just reading min/max at root) is O(1) but extracting (removing) is O(log n) because it triggers heapify.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Priority Queue

formulas.name: Decrease-Key in Binary Heap
formulas.latex: \text{Decrease-Key: } O(\log n) \quad (\text{sift-up after decreasing a key})
formulas.plain: Decrease-Key in a min-heap costs O(log n) — decrease the key then sift up to restore heap property.
formulas.whenToUse: Used in Dijkstra's and Prim's algorithms; important for their overall complexity analysis.

terms.means: number of elements in the heap

formulas.trap: Decrease-Key requires knowing the position of the element (requires an index map). Without it, finding the element is O(n).
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Priority Queue

formulas.name: Tree Edges and Nodes
formulas.latex: \text{A tree with } n \text{ nodes has exactly } n - 1 \text{ edges}
formulas.plain: A tree with n nodes has exactly n - 1 edges.
formulas.whenToUse: Any problem verifying a tree structure or counting edges from node count.

terms.means: number of nodes (vertices) in the tree

formulas.trap: Adding one more edge creates a cycle (no longer a tree). Removing one edge disconnects the tree.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Tree

formulas.name: Sum of Degrees in a Tree
formulas.plain: Sum of all node degrees in a tree = 2*(n-1)  (each of the n-1 edges contributes 2 to the degree sum).
formulas.whenToUse: Deriving number of nodes/edges from degree information in tree problems.

terms.means: degree (number of edges incident) to node v

formulas.trap: In a rooted tree, degree of root = number of children; degree of other nodes = number of children + 1 (for parent edge).
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Tree

formulas.name: k-ary Tree: Leaves vs Internal Nodes
formulas.plain: In a full k-ary tree (every internal node has exactly k children): L = (k-1)*I + 1
formulas.whenToUse: Generalizing the binary tree leaf/full-node relationship to k-ary trees.

terms.means: number of internal nodes (each with exactly k children)
branching factor (k=2 gives binary: L = I + 1)

formulas.trap: For k=2 this reduces to L = I + 1 (the standard formula). For k=3, L = 2I + 1. Apply carefully for ternary/quaternary trees.
formulas.reference: Programming and Data Structures / Tree
